questions.question: What does CODE stand for in the CODE-CODER framework?
options: Content, Operations & Martech, Design & Data, Engineering
questions.explanation: CODE captures the four domains that every digital build runs through: Content, Operations & Martech, Design & Data, Engineering.

questions.question: What does CODER stand for as the inner engineering letters?
options: Code, Optimization & Quality, Deployment, External integrations, Release management
questions.explanation: CODER zooms inside the Engineering letter: Code, Optimization & Quality, Deployment, External integrations, Release management.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a Daily Stand-up Meeting (DSM)?
options: Sprint planning for the upcoming week
Surfacing blockers fast and syncing the team
questions.explanation: DSMs are 15-minute daily rituals where each person answers three questions to surface blockers and sync the team — not status reporting.

questions.question: In RACI, who owns the outcome of an activity?
options: Responsible — the person doing the work
Informed — anyone kept in the loop
questions.explanation: Accountable owns the outcome — and there is only ever one Accountable person per activity. Responsible does the work; Consulted provides input; Informed is kept in the loop.

questions.question: What is the difference between a locale and a language in content localization?
options: Locales include country and culture; languages do not
Languages are paid; locales are free
Locales are technical; languages are creative
questions.explanation: en-IN and en-US share a language but differ in spelling, currency, regulation and cultural reference. Locales are planned as markets, not as translations.

questions.question: What is the role of an IA sitemap?
options: It's submitted to search engines for crawling
It defines the conceptual site structure and drives the content model
It tracks site traffic over time
It manages user permissions across the site
questions.explanation: The IA sitemap defines page types, hierarchy, and navigation — and drives the content model itself. The XML sitemap is the file submitted to search engines.

questions.question: What is a data layer in web analytics?
options: A backup database for analytics events
A JavaScript object on the page holding context for tags to read
The visualization layer in BI dashboards
A subscription tier in Adobe Analytics
questions.explanation: The data layer is a JavaScript object holding page, product, and user context. Analytics tools, tag managers, and personalization engines all read from it instead of scraping the DOM.

questions.question: What is the role of Adobe Target?
options: Track analytics events across the site
Decide what experience to show which user, and measure whether it worked
questions.explanation: Analytics tells you what happened; personalization decides what should happen next. Target picks the experience per segment and runs the A/B framework to measure outcomes.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a Customer Data Platform (CDP)?
options: Unify customer data into one profile per real person and activate downstream
questions.explanation: A CDP ingests data from every system, resolves identities, builds one unified profile per person, and activates segments in real time to downstream systems.

questions.question: What is content velocity?
options: The number of authors on a content team
How quickly content moves from idea to published
The page load speed of content pages
questions.explanation: Content velocity measures how quickly content moves from idea to published — cycle time per type, throughput per week. Often the difference between weekly and quarterly campaign cadence.

questions.question: Why does asset expiry matter in DAM?
options: Time-bound licences and releases create legal risk if expired assets stay live
questions.explanation: Stock licences, model releases, and brand-mark windows expire. Orphaned expired assets are legal risk — they need auto-alerts, renewal workflows, or retirement.

questions.question: What is the most common bottleneck in a content approval workflow?
questions.explanation: Gates without named owners and SLAs become the bottleneck. Anything unbounded — 'send to legal' with no deadline — silently dominates cycle time.

questions.question: What is GEO in the context of search optimization?
options: Generative Engine Optimization — optimizing for AI answer engines
Global Engagement Optimization for international traffic
questions.explanation: GEO is Generative Engine Optimization — optimizing content to be cited by AI answer engines like ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, and Google AI Overviews.

questions.question: What three questions does each person answer in a Daily Stand-up?
options: What I did yesterday, what I'll do today, what's blocking me
Hours worked, hours remaining, priority next
Team morale, technical debt, deployment plan
questions.explanation: Three questions, one purpose: surface blockers fast. The DSM is not status reporting; it's a sync ritual to unblock work.

questions.question: What are the five phases of campaign management?
questions.explanation: Plan → Build → Launch → Measure → Iterate. Work products differ by industry but the phases don't.

questions.question: In stakeholder expectation management, what is the 'triangle' principle?
options: Time, cost, quality — pick two; the third gives
Scope, schedule, stakeholders — balance all three equally
questions.explanation: Time, cost, quality — pick two; the third gives. Make stakeholders make the choice explicitly.

questions.question: What is the role of a tag manager (e.g., Adobe Launch, GTM)?
options: Manage analytics and marketing tags centrally without code deployments
questions.explanation: Tag managers like Adobe Launch and Google Tag Manager let non-developers configure analytics and marketing tags from one UI, decoupling tag changes from code deployments.

questions.question: In analytics terminology, what is a 'conversion'?
options: A desired action you're optimizing for (purchase, signup, lead form)
questions.explanation: A conversion is a desired action the business is optimising for — typically purchase, signup, or lead form submit. The funnel measures progress toward conversions.

questions.question: In the CODE-CODER framework, which letter telescopes inward into five sub-letters?
questions.explanation: Engineering (E) is the one that telescopes inward into CODER: Code, Optimization & Quality, Deployment, External integrations, Release management.

questions.question: When mapping content sources, what are the three questions every content type must answer?
options: Where does it come from, who provides it, in what format
Who owns it, what's the deadline, what's the budget
Is it new, is it relevant, is it on-brand
Is it text, image, or video
questions.explanation: Source, channel, and format. Answers belong in the discovery doc, not in someone's head.

questions.question: In campaign management, when should learnings be documented?
questions.explanation: Document learnings before the next plan starts — Iterate is a campaign phase, not an after-thought.

questions.question: What is a 'segment' in analytics?
options: A saved filter that defines a group of users
questions.explanation: A segment is a saved filter defining a group — e.g., 'Mobile users from IN who viewed a product but didn't purchase.' It can be rule-based or ML-based.

questions.question: What's the role of a controlled taxonomy in a DAM?
options: Reliable findability and consistent classification across teams
questions.explanation: A controlled hierarchical vocabulary (e.g., Vehicle > SUV > Compact) makes findability reliable and classification consistent. Free tags supplement; they never replace structure.

questions.question: Where does AI add value in asset management?
options: Auto-tagging, alt-text generation, semantic search, variant generation
questions.explanation: AI accelerates the manual taxonomy and findability work — auto-tagging, alt-text, captions, image generation for first drafts, variant resizing, and semantic search.

questions.question: Who is the primary audience for the CODE-CODER framework?
options: Architects and project managers who watch domains, not functions
questions.explanation: Architects and PMs watch domains — they need a letter-logic that scales from a line of code to a programme of work. CODE-CODER gives them that lens.

questions.question: Which five files form the core of the Stalwart framework?
questions.explanation: Stalwart's five core files: init.js (onLoad + storage), model.js (data structure), biz.js (events + decisions), api.js (calls + mapping), render.js (UI).

questions.question: What are the three optional files in the 8-file Stalwart structure?
questions.explanation: config.js (settings), state.js (persistent state), events.js (event vocabulary) are the three optional files. Add them when complexity demands; skip them when it doesn't.

questions.question: Where is Adobe Cloud Manager hosted?
options: On the customer's dedicated AMS tenant
As multi-tenant SaaS on Adobe's own cloud infrastructure
On AWS in the customer's account
On-premise at Adobe's data centres only
questions.explanation: Cloud Manager is a multi-tenant SaaS hosted by Adobe globally. Deployment targets (AEM Author, Publishers, Dispatchers) live in your dedicated AMS tenant; the control plane is shared, the data plane is not.

questions.question: What's the main architectural difference between Adobe Analytics (AA) and Customer Journey Analytics (CJA)?
options: AA is on-premise; CJA is cloud
AA is paid; CJA is free
AA is hit-based and page-centric; CJA is journey-based and person-centric, built on AEP with XDM
AA covers web only; CJA covers mobile only
questions.explanation: AA is hit-based, page-view-centric, built on eVars/props/events. CJA is journey-based, person-centric, built on Adobe Experience Platform with XDM schemas — joins web, app, call centre, in-store into one person.

questions.question: At 500 TPS target with a 90% dispatcher cache hit ratio, how much traffic reaches AEM Publishers?
questions.explanation: Cache hit ratio is the lever. At 90% hit, only 10% (50 TPS) reaches Publishers. At 50% hit, 250 TPS does. Plan capacity against the worst plausible cache miss scenario.

questions.question: What is the standard RPO for AMS Standard tier backups?
questions.explanation: Daily snapshots = 24h RPO on Standard tier. Higher tiers (Gold, Platinum) configure down to ~1h via transaction log shipping. Banking RTO=1h / RPO=15min usually requires custom DR architecture.

questions.question: Which Dynatrace integration option gives the deepest visibility into AEM MS?
options: Dynatrace OneAgent installed on AMS VMs (Adobe-approved)
API pull from Adobe-exposed metrics endpoints
questions.explanation: OneAgent installed on AMS VMs is the gold standard — full-stack APM, JVM metrics, GC, transaction tracing. Install requires Adobe approval and is treated as a managed software install.

questions.question: What authentication should AEM Publisher use when calling Oracle VBCS APIs?
options: OAuth 2.0 with IDCS tokens, optionally mTLS for banking
questions.explanation: OAuth 2.0 with Oracle IDCS tokens is the baseline. Add mTLS for banking. A dedicated service account, token caching, audit logging on both ends, and quarterly secret rotation complete the pattern.

questions.question: What is the tenant isolation model in AEM Managed Services?
options: Shared compute with logical separation only
Dedicated VPC, compute, network, and storage per customer; only Cloud Manager control plane and IMS identity are shared
All customers share the same VPC with security groups
Single-tenant only with full physical isolation
questions.explanation: AMS is managed dedicated infrastructure, not shared-tenancy SaaS. Each customer gets dedicated VPC, VMs, network, storage. Only orchestration (Cloud Manager) and identity (IMS) are shared services.

questions.question: What is the BYOK (Bring Your Own Key) status on Adobe AMS, historically?
options: Fully supported out of the box for all customers
Limited or no BYOK support historically; contract-specific; some recent configurations support customer-managed keys
Available only on AEM as a Cloud Service, never on AMS
questions.explanation: AMS has had limited BYOK support historically. Confirm in writing with Adobe. AEM as a Cloud Service has broader BYOK options. If BYOK isn't supported, compensating controls: data classification, tenant isolation, KMS audit logging, time-bounded operator access.

questions.question: In the AEM Banking defense-in-depth chain, what role does the AEM Dispatcher play?
options: Application-layer cache and URL filter — the AEM-native control for path, method, and parameter-based denials
questions.explanation: Dispatcher is layer 4 in the chain: an application-layer cache plus a URL filter — the AEM-native 'WAF lite' for path, method, and parameter-based denials. Sits between Adobe WAF and the Publishers.

questions.question: What sits at maturity rung 5 of the data maturity ladder?
options: Unified CDP (real-time profile, segments activated to every channel)
questions.explanation: Rung 5 is Unified CDP — one profile per real person, real-time segments activated to every channel. Rung 6 is Orchestrated Journeys with AJO + AI; rung 3 is Cross-channel Analytics with CJA.

questions.question: What is the 'mental model' in the anatomy of code?
options: The architecture diagram of the application
The idea that every line of code answers a question — when, where, what, how, group, decide, when-to-do
questions.explanation: Every line of code answers a question. When = trigger, where = function, what = logic, how = variables, group = model, decide = function, when-to-do = event-controller. The mental model orients reading and writing code.

questions.question: What are the three types of functions in the anatomy of code?
questions.explanation: Three function types: Logical (does the thinking), Render (does the showing), Feature (composes logical and render to deliver capability). Within each, you often see initialize() and go() patterns.

questions.question: In the events architecture, what is the role of a generic dispatcher?
options: A single hub that routes typed events from dispatch points to registered handlers, decoupling sender from receiver
questions.explanation: Generic dispatcher = central event bus. Code dispatches typed events; handlers register/listen by type. Decouples sender from receiver. Sort/filter operations sit alongside as collection helpers.

questions.question: What does 'decoupled parallel authoring' enable?
options: Multiple authors working simultaneously without blocking each other, increasing content velocity
questions.explanation: Parallel authoring without locks lets multiple contributors work simultaneously, reducing the longest-pole effect in cycle time. One of the key velocity levers alongside templated authoring and AI-assisted drafting.

questions.question: What's the recommended network segmentation for AEM AMS tiers?
options: All tiers in one subnet for simplicity
DMZ for AppGW/LB, Web tier for Dispatchers, App tier for Publishers, Secure tier for Author, Data tier for storage
Public subnet for Publishers, private for Author only
VPC peering across all tiers without subnet boundaries
questions.explanation: Five subnets: DMZ (AppGW), Web (Dispatchers), App (Publishers), Secure (Author, VPN-only), Data (object stores, backup). NSGs deny-all by default; explicit allow rules per port and source; no skip-tier paths.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Architect's Review pattern in the framework?
options: A code review by senior architects only
A structured checklist run before anything ships — questions across all CODE-CODER letters that prove an engagement is honest
questions.explanation: Architect's Review is a pre-ship checklist asking pointed questions across the framework — content model, ops cadence, data lineage, engineering hygiene, integrations. It surfaces what would otherwise come up on the deploy weekend.

questions.question: Under DPDP, what's a defensible propagation latency budget for consent opt-out signals?
options: Within 15 minutes across all consuming systems
questions.explanation: A reasonable target is 'all systems reflect opt-out within 15 minutes of capture.' Monitor the propagation pipeline; alarm on lag; build a consent kill switch for emergency suspension if the pipeline fails.

questions.question: What are the three governance layers for Azure OpenAI in production?
options: Free tier, paid tier, enterprise tier
Azure service limits (TPM/RPM), application-level controls (API Gateway, per-user budgets, semantic cache), and cost governance (budgets, alerts, kill switch)
questions.explanation: Three layers: (1) Azure-side TPM/RPM quotas plus PTU reservations; (2) app-level rate limiting via API Gateway, per-user budgets, prompt length caps, semantic cache; (3) cost governance: budgets, alerts, kill switch, audit logging.

questions.question: What is XDM in the context of Customer Journey Analytics?
options: Experience Data Model — Adobe's standardized schema for cross-channel data on AEP
questions.explanation: Experience Data Model is the standardised schema sitting on Adobe Experience Platform. CJA uses XDM to join data from any source — web, app, call centre, in-store — under a unified person view.

questions.question: Why must the IA sitemap live next to the design system rather than in a slide deck?
options: Because authors will invent structure ad-hoc otherwise, and the content model fractures within two quarters
questions.explanation: Without a living IA sitemap, authors invent structure ad-hoc. The content model fractures, downstream teams build workarounds, and re-platforming becomes the only fix. The IA sitemap is documentation that authors actually use.

questions.question: Why is identity stitching critical in a CDP?
options: To resolve that user@gmail.com on web is the same person as loyalty card #12345 in-store — unifying disparate identifiers into one profile
questions.explanation: Every system has its own identifier — email, cookie, loyalty ID, phone. Identity stitching resolves these into one profile per real person. Without it, the CDP just stores fragmented identities, not unified profiles.

questions.question: What's the standard pattern for Akamai-to-Adobe-AMS connectivity?
options: HTTPS over public internet with Akamai egress IP allow-listing on Adobe MS firewall, plus origin authentication (signed headers or mTLS)
Direct fibre between Akamai POPs and Adobe data centres
questions.explanation: AMS doesn't offer private peering to third-party CDNs. Standard pattern: HTTPS over internet + IP allow-list (Akamai's published egress IPs) + origin authentication. Track the IP allowlist owner — most common operational gap.

questions.question: Why does the anatomy of code start from 'every line answers a question'?
options: To orient reading and writing code around intent — turning code from instructions into answers, which makes both review and authorship more deliberate
Because every line must have a comment
questions.explanation: When every line answers a question, reading code becomes asking questions and writing code becomes answering them. That reframing changes both review and authorship — from 'is the syntax right' to 'is this answering the right question well'.

questions.question: In Cloud Manager, what is the purpose of a Front-End pipeline compared to a Full-Stack pipeline?
options: Front-End pipelines deploy AEM bundles; Full-Stack pipelines deploy JavaScript only
Front-End pipelines build and deploy UI resources (JS, CSS, clientlibs) independently of AEM code, enabling faster iterations without a full AEM release cycle
They are identical — Front-End is just a legacy label
Front-End pipelines run in staging only; Full-Stack pipelines reach production
questions.explanation: Decoupling front-end from full-stack lets UX teams iterate at their own cadence — a CSS change no longer needs a full AEM code deployment. This is the key DEPT® delivery pattern for AEM Sites projects.

questions.question: What is the primary risk of a blue-green deployment strategy, and how is it mitigated?
options: Database schema drift between blue and green — mitigated by backward-compatible migrations run before the switch
DNS propagation lag — mitigated by deploying both environments in the same region
Doubled memory usage — mitigated by auto-scaling both environments simultaneously
Cache invalidation — mitigated by disabling CDN during the switch
questions.explanation: Blue-green requires both environments to be able to run against the same database simultaneously during the switch window. Backward-compatible migrations (expand / contract pattern) prevent schema drift from breaking either environment.

questions.question: Which three signal types form the foundation of an observability stack?
questions.explanation: Metrics tell you something is wrong, logs tell you what happened, traces tell you where. All three are needed — alerts fire on metrics, but you diagnose with logs and traces. Uptime/Latency/Error Rate are the classic SLIs derived from metrics, not the signals themselves.

questions.question: What distinguishes Infrastructure as Code (IaC) from a runbook of manual provisioning steps?
options: IaC is faster to run but produces the same outcome
IaC is version-controlled, idempotent, and diffable — making it reviewable, repeatable, and recoverable in a way a runbook cannot be
IaC only works on cloud; runbooks work on-prem
IaC eliminates the need for approval workflows
questions.explanation: The real value of IaC is that infrastructure enters the same engineering discipline as code: peer review, version history, diff on change, automated testing. Idempotency means running it twice produces the same result — a runbook cannot guarantee that.

questions.question: A deployment pipeline fails at the integration-test stage on every Friday afternoon push. The root cause is most likely:
options: A flaky test that depends on external state (time, shared test data, or a downstream service in an inconsistent state)
A conflict in the Git merge strategy
questions.explanation: Day-of-week failure patterns almost always indicate environment-dependent flakiness — a shared test database that another team's Friday batch job dirtied, a downstream staging service that gets rebooted on Fridays, or a time-sensitive assertion. Fix: isolate test data and mock or contract-test external dependencies.

questions.question: In a containerised deployment, what is the correct way to handle application secrets?
options: Bake secrets into the Docker image at build time for reliability
Pass secrets as environment variables injected at runtime from a secret store (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Kubernetes Secrets)
Store secrets in the Git repo in a .secrets file with restricted permissions
Hard-code secrets in the entrypoint script inside the container
questions.explanation: Secrets baked into images are a critical vulnerability — images are often pushed to registries, cached, and shared. Runtime injection from a managed secret store keeps the secret out of version control, out of image layers, and auditable. Kubernetes Secrets or a dedicated vault are the industry standard.

questions.question: What does a shift-left testing strategy mean in a delivery pipeline context?
options: Running all tests on the left-hand side of the Kanban board
Moving quality checks earlier in the pipeline — linting, unit tests, and security scans run before integration or deployment stages, catching defects closer to the code change
Removing manual QA from the process entirely
Running tests in parallel to reduce pipeline duration
questions.explanation: Shift-left reduces the cost of defects by finding them earlier. A bug caught at lint time costs seconds to fix; the same bug found in UAT costs days. The pattern: pre-commit hooks → PR checks → unit → integration → E2E → production smoke test — each stage adds confidence and cost.

questions.question: What is the main operational advantage of feature flags over branch-based feature releases?
options: Feature flags make code run faster
Feature flags decouple deployment from release — code ships to production continuously but features activate on demand, enabling rollback without a redeploy
Feature flags eliminate the need for testing
Feature flags enforce access control on the codebase
questions.explanation: Trunk-based development + feature flags is the modern continuous delivery pattern. Code goes to production every day; a flag controls whether users see the feature. Rolling back means toggling a flag, not reverting a deployment. This is how organisations like DEPT® can ship to AEMaaCS dozens of times per week safely.

questions.question: In an AEM + CDN architecture, which cache invalidation strategy has the lowest risk of serving stale content after a content publish?
options: TTL-based expiry with a 24-hour cache lifetime
Tag-based invalidation — pages and assets tagged at publish time, flushed precisely when the underlying content changes
Manual cache purge by the operations team
Setting Cache-Control: no-store on all AEM responses
questions.explanation: Tag-based invalidation (Surrogate-Key / Cache-Tag headers, supported by Fastly, Cloudflare, and Akamai) lets you flush exactly the pages that contain a changed content fragment without purging unrelated content. TTL alone always has a staleness window; manual purge is error-prone and slow; no-store defeats the CDN entirely.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a Software Composition Analysis (SCA) scan in a CI pipeline?
options: To analyse how code is structured and suggest refactoring
To detect known vulnerabilities (CVEs) in third-party dependencies and licencing conflicts before they reach production
To measure code coverage across the test suite
questions.explanation: SCA tools (Snyk, Dependabot, OWASP Dependency-Check) scan your package.json / pom.xml / requirements.txt against CVE databases. Catching a vulnerable transitive dependency at PR time is far cheaper than responding to a security incident in production. This is a mandatory gate in any mature DevOps pipeline.
